title: Just Let Go
original_prose:
“Woke up today and decided to kill my ego / It ain't ever done me no good no how”

— Sturgill Simpson, Just Let Go

####

[CLASSIFIED — SYNRGY INTERNAL DISPATCH]ORIGIN: SYNRGY ONE COMMAND — SynRgy OneTO: CENTRAL STRATEGIC AUTHORITY

Weekly broadcast interference from gridpoint DEN-04 persists.

Despite routine airstrikes, insurgent activity resumes within 48 hours. Ground teams have failed to locate rebel entrenchments—suspect subterranean cover or decoy routing.

Request escalation protocol:Recommend deployment of two (2) low-yield CORE devices. Proven effective in urban entrenchment purges—see BEI-09, LAX-03.

Awaiting authorization.— Cmdr. V. Halden, 6th Bombardment Wing



####

The void’s smaller now.

It used to stretch. Breathe. Pretend it was a place. Now it just flickers. Like it’s remembering how to die.

We sit where we always sit. Stone beneath us. Or what used to be stone. What used to be real.

The sword sits beside me.

I want to throw it. Gods, I want to.

It hums faintly. Not like before. Not like it did when it thought I needed it. Just a low murmur. A reminder.

He glances at it, then back at me. Doesn’t ask.

“It’s still here,” I say.

“It’s waiting,” he says.

“I know.”

I don’t pick it up. I don’t touch it. But I feel it in my bones, like a bruise that hasn’t decided if it’s healing or breaking open again.

I want to let it go. Not yet.

He’s older again. Taller maybe. The shape of him more certain.

We’re laughing. Just a little.

Not because anything’s funny. Because we survived. That used to mean something.

He watches me. Same way he always does. Like he’s waiting for me to name something I never learned the word for.

“That moment,” he says, voice steady. “Her on top of the truck. That seems important.”

“It was.”

“You didn’t know it then.”

“No. I didn’t.”

He nods. The light flickers again. Not starlight. Just… failure. A sun somewhere gasping its last breath.

“We don’t have long,” I say.

“No,” he agrees.

I close my eyes. See it again. Her, barefoot and furious. Blood and smoke and that cable hook gleaming like a crown.

“They watched her,” I say.

“They believed her,” he says.

"I believed her. In her."

And then the silence comes. That thick, warm kind. The kind that happens right before something is born.

I open my eyes.

The last sun winks out.

We’re almost done.



####

The box is too big for a grave.

We unhooked it anyway. Rolled it back from the truck and left it where the land fell away—into a ravine full of ash and brittle sage.

The bodies inside didn’t rot. Not fast, anyway. Two days in the heat and they’d started to turn—sweet and sharp, bloating in places. I wanted to dump them the second we cleared the city line. Let the coyotes sort it out.

But she said no.

Said she knew a place. Somewhere proper. Not sacred—just… right.

They had been food.

Bound for the reclamation plant behind SynRgy One. Meat harvest. Spark-cauterized and slotted for protein gels. We cracked the locks, climbed over them, and made our escape in a truck full of corpses that deserved better.

Kat stands at the edge. Arms crossed. Hair matted from sweat and sleep.

She doesn’t speak right away. Just looks down into the pit, lips pressed tight.

Then:

"We’re sorry."

It’s soft. Almost too soft to hear.

"You didn’t ask to be part of this. You didn’t choose how your bodies were used. You didn't ask to be used as weapons... as energy."

She glances at me. Quick. Sharp.

"Neither did some of us."

I nod. It’s fair.

"I shouldn’t have triggered the core," she says, still to the dead. "It wasn’t supposed to go off like that. But maybe… maybe that’s what mercy looks like now."

We drop the lid. It lands with a hollow thud. Dust swallows it.

We scavenged on the way. Pulled clothes from cars and closets. She found a dress under a pile of half-burned mail. I got out of that bloody uniform. No insignia now. No name.

She’s barefoot again. Always is. Even with boots in her hand.

"I like to feel the ground," she says. "It’s the only thing that still feels real."

We sit on a ledge above the grave for a while. Just watching the wind try to remember how to move.

The highway below is empty. Has been since Nevada.

"You notice," I say, "we haven’t seen a single soul out here? No scavengers. No checkpoints. Not even a bird."

"Yeah."

"Was it the war?"

She shakes her head.

"No. It’s the grid. SynRgy pulled every soul and body out of places like this. Fed 'em into Heaven High and left the dirt hollow. Ain’t just the war. It’s hunger dressed up like salvation."

We don’t talk after that.

Not for a long time.

We just rest.

Long enough to believe we might deserve to.



####

A half-buried sign reads: RED ROCKS AMPHITHEATRE — EVENT SCHEDULE CANCELED.

The rocks rise like teeth. Old gods with broken backs.

We drive in slow. No one waves.

Kat sits forward, elbows on knees, eyes locked on the ridgeline above the road. She doesn’t speak. Doesn’t smile. But I can feel it in her—the hum. She knows this place. It knows her.

When the checkpoint appears, it isn’t a gate. It’s three rifles and a voice like dust.

“Names.”

Kat leans out. “Tell Echo the bitch is back.”

A pause.

Then a low laugh. “Well I’ll be damned.”

The rifles lower. One disappears into a crevice. A moment later, the mountain opens.

Not a cave. A city.

They’ve carved homes and halls and whole lives into the stone. Buried deep under the ruins of the amphitheater. There’s light down there—warm and flickering, not electric. Smoke coils out of vents. Music hums through the rocks like breath.

We descend.

They start to gather.

Survivors. Scarred but breathing. Their clothes are mismatched, half-mended, scorched in places. Faces lean. Eyes sharp. But there's color in their cheeks. Not from makeup. From sun and salt and hunger that hasn’t yet won.

Compared to the glass-eyed slaves in SynRgy One? They’re gods.

Not clean. Not safe. But free. In the way only rebels in a dying revolution ever are.

They move with purpose, not programming. Some carry tools. Some, weapons. A few wear old uniforms stripped of rank, sleeves torn, shoulders bare.

And there are children.

That hits harder than I expect.

Small hands covered in chalk and ash. Girls chasing boys in wide loops through the stone corridors. One group dragging a busted drone on a rope like a trophy. No one stops them. No one scolds.

Where I’m from, that would’ve gotten a family re-educated.

Here? It’s just joy.

Not innocence. That’s gone. But defiance dressed up like childhood.

They aren’t the good guys.

I don’t think there are good guys anymore.

But they’re better. I know that much.

Some run to her. Some stop and stare. A few reach out to touch her like she might vanish.

She nods. Keeps moving. Not cold—just practiced.

Then they see me.

The shift is instant. Smiles vanish. Hands tighten around straps and blades. One woman spits in the dirt.

We keep walking.

Then a voice behind us:

“Hold up.”

Boots. Heavy. Sure.

He’s taller than I remember. Broader. Hair shaved close, old scars on his scalp like lightning maps. Still carries the same lancer rig across his back like it’s a damn flag.

“Tell me I’m seeing things,” he says, eyes locked on me.

Kat steps between us.

“Heya, Bambi. How's it going?"

“Tell me you didn’t bring that SynRgy motherfucker into my house.”

“I did.”

Silence. The kind that makes fingers twitch near triggers.

“Heard about Vegas,” Bambi says. “Heard what he did. What you did.”

Kat doesn’t move. “You heard the end. Not the road.”

Bambi looks at me. No hate. Just calculation.

“You with them still?”

“No,” I say.

“You loyal to her?”

I don’t answer fast enough.

Kat’s voice cuts sharp: “He is.”

Another pause.

Bambi nods slow. One time.

“Then you’re lucky,” he says, stepping back. “'Cause I was about to redecorate this whole corridor with your teeth.”

Weapons lower.

But not the eyes.

They don’t trust me.

Good.

Neither do I.

We walk deeper into the stone.



####

We pass doorways carved by hand, firelight behind canvas flaps. The smell of broth. Sweat. Earth. It's a city built on memory and mud and spite. The kind of place that shouldn’t exist, but does anyway—because it has to.

They cheer when they see her.

Kat doesn’t slow. Doesn’t wave. Just keeps walking like she’s late to something more important.

"You’re the leader now," I murmur.

She snorts. Doesn’t look at me. "All the leaders are long gone. We’re just us."

Someone tosses her a canteen. She catches it without breaking stride, drinks, throws it back. There’s a rhythm to how she moves here. A pattern.

She belongs.

I don’t.

A few people nod to me. Most just glare. One kid throws a pebble. Misses. Barely.

We wind through a tunnel slick with condensation and torch soot. Stone gives way to structure—old concert gear repurposed as barricades and scaffolding. Banners made of torn tents and signage hang from rebar crossbeams. Everything hums.

She holds my hand like we’re children. And then she doesn’t. Then she wraps her arm in mine and clutches it like a lifeline.

I know it’s a trick. She’s not scared. She’s setting a tone.

I need her. But she’s already whole without me.

And I don’t know if she feels the same. And I'm okay with what I have.

We reach a split in the path. A rusted-out dressing room door leans against the wall, painted with some kind of sigil. Beyond it, laughter. Warm. Rough.

She stops. Turns to me.

“You’re not coming in.”

Before I can speak, three women step out of the room. All Echo. All muscle and mischief.

One grins. "Nuh-uh, big boy. You don’t get to see her right now."

Another: "You can see her later tonight. At the show."

Kat lights up. "Is that tonight? We made it in time?"

The third: "Damn right you did, girlfriend. Now your Captain America can piss right off until curtain call. Got it?"

Kat shrugs at me. What can you do?

The women usher her inside. The door swings shut.

Laughter fades.

I’m alone again.

But for the first time in a long time, I don’t mind.



####

I don’t wander far.

Red Rocks below is warmer than it should be. It smells like wood smoke and stew. Fried onions. Boiled socks. Home.

I walk the upper corridors, past makeshift clinics, prayer circles lit by candles, schools scratched into amphitheater stone. There’s a rhythm here. Familiar, but wrong. Like a hymn sung in a different key.

I turn a corner and nearly collide with him.

Bambi.

A brick wall in boots. Skin like carved obsidian. Scars everywhere the armor didn’t cover. He walks like a tank that learned how to pray. Too big for the tunnels, too loud for stealth. But no one tells him to be smaller.

He makes me nervous.

Not because he’s dangerous. Though he is.

Because I was trained to see men like him as threats. Targets. Something between riot and resource.

But that broke with Kat.

She shattered whatever programming they buried in me the moment she bled on that hangar floor and laughed through it. Now Bambi’s just a man. One who’d die for her. One who might kill for me—if I don’t fuck it up. But that didn’t start with Kat.

It cracked open the night we danced in Kansas.

I remember the sweat. The laughter. The way the stars hung too low like they were watching something holy.

I remember thinking: this is what freedom feels like. Not the kind they preached. The kind that didn’t need a badge or a banner.

That night broke me open.

Kat just finished the job.

"You lost, Captain America?"

I shake my head. "Just walking."

He snorts. Then walks with me.

We don’t talk at first. Just walk the rim of the hollowed-out city. From above, I can see them all. Blacks. Browns. The kind of people I was trained to patrol. To profile. To pity. All of them alive in ways I never was.

"This isn’t what I expected," I say.

"What’d you expect? Huts and hand drums? Savage mutiny?"

"I don’t know. Something else."

Bambi stops. Leans on a railing made from busted guitar stands. "We’ve got medics down there that used to be surgeons. Engineers building hydroponics from scavenged strip lights. That woman running the nursery? Used to be VP of Comms at a SynRyg."

"And the kids?"

He smiles. "They’re ours. We don’t breed them to serve. We raise them to resist."

Below us, a girl with no shoes and half a mohawk chases a boy wearing a SynRgy pilot’s helmet like a crown. They laugh. They’re free. Not clean. Not safe. But free.

I shift. The air feels thinner here.

"You uncomfortable?"

"A little."

"Why?"

I pause. "Because I was supposed to hate this."

He nods slow. "And now?"

"Now I just hate what told me to."

He claps a hand on my shoulder. It’s not light. It’s not gentle. But it’s not a threat.

"If you hurt her," he says again, quiet now, "I'll reach through your throat and pull out your asshole through your lungs. Understand?"

I nod. "I didn’t mean to step on something. You two… were a thing?"

He shakes his head. "No. We were never a thing."

He leans forward a little, not angry, just clear. "She’s been too busy fighting you fuckers—no offense—to have a thing."

"None taken."

"She’s family. You understand that? Not blood. Deeper than that. And if you hurt her, I will kill you."

"I understand."

"Good," he says, straightening. "Now let’s go watch the show."



####

The sky opens as we climb.

Red stone everywhere. Like walking through the inside of a throat. We emerge high above the stage, where the air tastes cleaner and older. Sunlight angles low, catching dust in beams. People are already gathering in the carved seats—clusters of bodies wrapped in patchwork blankets, guitars in laps, soft voices tuning strings.

I squint at the crowd. There are hundreds.

“What's going on?” I ask.

Bambi doesn’t look at me.

“Just watch.”

The amphitheater is alive. Families sit cross-legged near the edges. Children bounce on stone steps. A group of teens pass around a battered boombox wrapped in copper wire. The music fades. A hush ripples through the crowd.

“Please turn off all artificial lights,” a voice says through a rusted PA. “Candles only from this point.”

A soft wave of fire kindles from the stage outward—small flames cupped in jars, old wine bottles, the hollowed-out base of a SynRgy surveillance pod. It’s beautiful. Fierce. Stolen light.

The sun sinks low.

And then she walks out.

Kat.

The lights dim further. A soft scratch comes through the speakers, then a voice, deep and velvet with old radio rust:

"And now—straight from a tour of kicking ass and taking names in the heart of the devil’s own empire... put your damn hands together for the one, the only—Madeleine Josette Baptiste-Laveau!"

Cheers rise. Not wild. Not forced. Reverent.

Barefoot. Dressed in that black mink skirt someone must’ve saved for something important. Hair loose. Shoulders square. Eyes scanning like she’s bracing for sniper fire.

She steps up to the mic. Breathes once. Finds me in the crowd.

"Thank you, AJ," she says, her voice slipping into that soft bayou rhythm, smooth and grounded.

The crowd simmers, leaning in like it’s gospel time.

"Y’all hush now. I ain’t done nothin’ none of you wouldn’t have done given the same chance."

Scattered claps. A whistle. She holds up a hand.

"Yeah, I ain’t gonna lie. Felt good, didn’t it? Stickin’ it to them in their own house? SynRgy thought they built a temple. We made it a tomb."

The crowd roars.

She waits.

Then:

"But I killed innocents, too. Don’t clap for that. Don’t you dare."

Silence sharpens.

"I know this is war. I know that happens. But we gotta remember: not everyone inside them towers asked for this. Some were just stuck. Some were just scared."

She breathes deep.

"Only thing I can say, for them, is this—we didn’t let SynRgy take their Sparks."

The crowd explodes. Deafening. Foot stomps, fists pounding stone. Someone sobs.

She holds fast.

"We gave ’em a dignified death. And that’s more than you can say for Voss."

Another wave. This one full of fury.

She closes her eyes. "Now. Moment of silence. For them. For all the lost. For the ones that came before us, and the ones that’ll carry the sword when we’re dust."

Candles flicker. No one speaks.

I lean over.

"I thought her name was Kat."

Bambi grins without looking at me. "Are you fuckin’ stupid, Captain America?"

I raise an eyebrow.

"Kat’s what we call her."

"Why?"

"You ever seen a pit bull latch on to somethin’ and not let go?"

"Yeah."

"It’s like that."

I’m flummoxed. Caught somewhere between pride and guilt and something I don’t have a word for.

But there’s no chance to ask for an explanation. The music is already starting again. The crowd is roaring. And she’s already turning toward the band, barefoot in the firelight, ready to burn the night down one verse at a time.

She steps back to the mic.

"And I know what some of y’all are thinkin’. That I wouldn’t’ve had to fight my way outta old Las Vegas if it wasn’t for the white boy."

The entire crowd turns to look at me.

I feel every set of eyes like a blade.

"You right," she says. "So damn right."

Beat.

"But maybe… maybe that’s how it had to go down. Maybe somethin’ in him snapped. And maybe somethin’ in us did, too. I don’t know."

She exhales.

"And tonight? I don’t wanna know. I don’t wanna think. I wanna sing. I wanna dance. I want us to scream at whatever gods are left that we’re still fuckin’ here."

The crowd roars. I roar.

She grabs the mic stand, leans in with that crooked, dangerous smile.

“This is for you, Captain America,” she says.

And then she sings.

I've never heard the song.

I'm so tiredOf playingPlaying with this bow and arrowGonna give my heart awayLeave it to the other girls to playFor I've been a temptress too long

JustGive me a reasonTo love youGive me a reason to beA woman

I just want to be a womanFrom this time, unchainedWe're all looking at a different picture

Her voice wobbles at first, like she doesn’t quite trust it to hold. But the band catches her. The crowd holds its breath. She leans into it. And the whole canyon leans with her.

JustGive me a reasonTo love youGive me a reason to beA woman

I just want to be a womanFrom this time, unchainedWe're all looking at a different pictureThrough this new frame of mindA thousand flowers could bloomMove over, and give us some room, yeah

She stared right at me. There was no spotlight, but it felt like there was.

Give me a reasonTo love youGive me a reason to beA woman

I just want to be a woman

So don't you stopBeing a manJust take a little lookFrom outside when you canSow a little tendernessNo matter if you cry

I think my heart stopped beating. Bambi slapped me on the back, as if I was choking.

Give me a reasonTo love youGive me a reason to beA womanI just want to be a woman

It's all I want to beIs all womanFor this is the beginningOf foreverAnd ever

It's time to move overIt's all I wanna be

I'm so tiredOf playingPlaying with this bow and arrowGonna give my heart awayLeave it to the other girls to playFor I've been a temptress too longJust

Give me a reason, to love you

The canyon moves like breath. The crowd sways with the beat—soft and slow at first, then deeper. Fuller. Couples dance in the aisles. Men with men. Women with women. Black and brown, white and gold. Hands around waists, fingers brushing arms, eyes closed like the war paused for them.

It’s not choreographed. It’s not sanctioned. It’s joy, feral and alive. I’ve never seen anything like it.

SynRgy taught us order. Rows and roles. Segregation disguised as optimization. Men stood with men only to fight. Women were cataloged and counted. There were rules for desire.

Here, there’s none of that.

Here, there’s just love.

The music swells. Her voice fades into the stone. Applause erupts—not sharp or frantic. Just loud and long and full of something sacred.

Kat bows.

Then she walks forward—slow and steady, through the glow of firelight and candle smoke. She shakes hands. Touches shoulders. Hugs a child who clutches her around the waist.

But her eyes never leave me.

And in that moment, I forget the war. I forget the blood. I forget everything but her.

She walks like the earth moves to let her pass.

And I’m the only thing in the world worth walking toward.



####

The crowd roars around us. She doesn’t stop. Just steps right into me like we never left SynRgy One. Like the bombs never fell. Like the blood never dried.

“I don’t know what to say,” I whisper.

She smiles. Crooked. Lit from within. "Then use your lips for somethin’ else."

She kisses me.

It’s not soft. It’s not sweet. It’s survival. It’s fury. It’s everything we didn’t have time for.

A voice breaks through the roar.

"Bombers incoming. Prepare for the show."

I pull back. Panic flares. "We have to get out of here."

She holds my face in both hands. "No, Captain America. Watch."

The sky begins to hum.

Three lights. Then six. Black shapes over the ruins of old Denver. They move slow. Deliberate. Heavy with ritual.

Then they start to drop.

Not firebombs. Not COREs.

Just old-fashioned, dumb steel. BOOM. BOOM. BOOM. A rhythm. A hymn.

The crowd doesn’t scatter. They cheer.

They raise fists. They scream. They cry and kiss and laugh.

"I don't understand. There's nothing there to bomb."

"Yeah, they don't know that. We put transmitters in the ruins on Fridays. Broadcast dumb shit—propaganda, whatever. They bomb on Saturday. We get a show."

Kat grabs my hand and pulls me toward the rim of the canyon. A quiet ledge above the chaos.

We fall into each other.

Not desperation. Not conquest.

Just gravity.

At first I’m too rough. Muscle memory. War.

She puts a hand on my chest. "No, Captain America. Everything ain't a nail needs a hammer."

She slips out of her dress. Climbs onto me. Hair backlit by fire. Skin lit by ordinance.

She is art. Sacred and unashamed.

I hesitate. The world is watching.

Then I see them.

All around us. Other bodies. Other pairs. Moving together in shadow and flame. No shame. No fear.

Just life refusing to vanish.

I let go.

We don’t fuck. We don’t make love.

We remember how to live.

Her breath in my ear. The world ending just outside the frame.

Later, I watch her sleep. A blanket pulled over her hip. The sky still lit with smoke and thunder.

She wasn’t a symbol.

She wasn’t mine.

She was what came next.

But I know this isn't the end. I know this isn't the happy ending.

Because my sword remembers.
